items.title: How a weight loss drug might give Denmark leverage in Trumps bid for Greenland | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The President of the European Council emphasized that Denmark's territorial integrity is a "crucial issue" for the European Union. This statement came in response to repeated comments from US President Donald Trump, who has expressed interest in acquiring Greenland and has not ruled out using force. Greenland, an autonomous territory within the Kingdom of Denmark, is at the center of this dispute.
Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen received support from European allies during visits to Berlin and Paris this week. She has consistently stated that the island is not for sale. A recent survey shows that 85% of Greenlanders oppose becoming part of the United States.

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The German parliament has been discussing proposals to prohibit the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party.
Germany's domestic intelligence agency is monitoring the AfD due to concerns that it may pose a threat to democracy. items.title: Trump and Putin: Will they make a deal at Ukraine’s expense? | To the point
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Russia has been waging a war of aggression against Ukraine for almost three years. Now, possible negotiations between U.S. President Donald Trump and Russia’s Vladimir Putin are raising the prospects of a peace deal. But would it be one that’s fair to Ukraine? Trump says he wants to meet Putin as soon as possible to find an end to the war. Putin, for his part, says he’s ready to negotiate – How seriously to take that? Well, Russian troops are still advancing in eastern Ukraine – and the question of how long the Ukrainians can hold out is becoming increasingly urgent. President Volodymyr Zelensky is demanding security guarantees for Ukraine. The topic: Trump and Putin: Will they make a deal at Ukraine’s expense?

Our guests: 
Gesine Dornblüth, Journalist
Vladimir Esipov, DW
Jessica Berlin, CEPA

02:08 The change of leadership in Washington could mean a new dynamic

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Serbia's Prime Minister has resigned after months of demonstrations across the country.
Milos Vucevic announced he was stepping down with an appeal for calm. items.title: Will China's DeepSeek burst the American AI bubble? | DW News Desk
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Chinese artificial intelligence company DeepSeek and its latest open-source large language model (LLM) are sending shockwaves through the world's tech industry. DeepSeek's LLM appears to match or even beat its competitors at a fraction of the cost. And DeepSeek is by far not the only AI model coming out of China. Alibaba's Qwen and Bytedance's Doubao are other examples of competitors catching up with American companies like OpenAI. US President Donald Trump says it's a 'wake-up call' for Silicon Valley, but how real is DeepSeek's AI revolution?  Can this Chinese startup truly overtake the US tech giants spending billions to win the AI race?

items.title: How are returning Syrians being received by the people that stayed in the country? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>EU foreign ministers meeting in Brussels have agreed on a plan to ease sanctions on Syria following the ousting of the Assad regime.

The bloc's foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said the plan would be swiftly implemented to help kickstart Syria's recovery after almost 14 years of civil war. But she said it could be reversed if the new government in Damascus does not respect human rights. Current sanctions include a ban on Syrian oil exports and financial restrictions. 

During Assad's regime, millions of Syrians left the country. Many of them are returning home for the first time in a decade. DW colleague Rama Jarmakani is one of them.
